LOS ANGELES, Cal, July g.
Mental pictures Of dignified. earn
est-faced orator, pleading with his
fellow men to leave off the evils OF
strong drink; long files of wretched
liquor-sated mortals who could
once boast of being respectable,
useful men slowly making their
way to the front of the church to
sign the famous pledge of Francis
Murphy-such are the stirring, I'M
pressive flashlights of Renaissance
called up to the little group OF
temperance workers in the Murphy
home here.


Every day for week they gath
cred in the hope they would be on
hand to offer a consoling word to
the wife and children when the
end came. Each day the physi-
clans declared the patient could
not possibly live more than a few
hours more. Still Francis Murphy
lived on, hour after hour, day after
day, every now and then Tightening
the silence of the deathroom with
SOME UTSeZy WlttiIloL thot hN'N>
him world-famed as an orator.


over 100000 great-hearted
men have signed my pledge in this
or other countries" was the sub
stance of one of DR.. Murphy's al
lusions to his life work. Those
who have not wavered will not
waver after my death, and those
who have stumbled must get to
their feet"


AT another time before his death
the patient said:


God is SO willing to look out
for the drunkard, II the latter will
only let him. There are worse
things than drinking. The man
who lusts for power, the inquisitive
spirit, the greedy man, are all
worse than the drunkard."


Francis Murphy spent a half
century of his 70 years for the
drinking man. Twelve million men
signed his pledge. He addressed
$4,000 meetings and visited thou
sands of saloons in quest of the
class he desired to save. In his
temperance work he save. In his
temperance work he Travelled L.
100000 miles, or, in other words,
stretched the piue ribbon of So
briefly 40 times around the globe.


Murphy was generous, jovial,
careless and hospitable. 1t may
not be wondered that the drink
habit became so well fastened to
him not long after his arrival in
this country al the age of Is. He
was fast going to the dogsa t Port
land, Me-, when rescued at the
very brink Of disaster by Captain
Sturtevant, master of sailing
schooner. Although he had little
education, Murphy scrubbed in a
little notebook this now famous
pledge, which has stood the test or
a generation unchanged:


with malice toward none, with
charity for all, | hereby pledge my
sacred honor that, God helping me,
I will abstain from all Intoxicating
liquors as beverage, and that l
will encourage others to abstain.
(Signed) Francis Murphy"


From that moment Murphy be-
came a character. At the end of a
month he had 100 names signed in
the little book to the pledge. His
